retrieved_docs.title: Open Angle Glaucoma -- Pathophysiology -- Lamina Cribrosa
retrieved_docs.content: The lamina cribrosa is a sieve-like fenestration at the back of the sclera that allows for a conglomeration of retinal axons and blood vessels to exit the posterior of the eye. Changes in the three-dimensional structure of the lamina cribrosa have been implicated in the pathogenesis of glaucomatous optic atrophy. [45] The superior and inferior channels of the lamina cribrosa contain larger pores and thinner connective tissue support for the passage of nerve-fiber bundles than the nasal and temporal parts of the lamina. The most common pattern of glaucomatous optic nerve thinning occurs at the inferior and superior portions of the optic nerve. Also, the superior and inferior laminar zones of the lamina cribrosa are where the arcuate area ganglion cell axons travel, and these axons are the most susceptible to glaucomatous damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Adaptive Optics -- Clinical Significance -- Lamina cribrosa
retrieved_docs.content: The visualization of lamina cribrosa, along with quantifying parameters like pore size, shape, and density, has been made possible by adaptive optics with good repeatability. Quantification and standardization of these parameters and correlation with predisposing genes may help screen and detect susceptible individuals at a very early stage. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Orbicularis Oris Muscle -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The muscle is a derivative of the mesoderm of the second branchial arch. [8] Between the 3rd and 8th week, mesoderm of the second arch starts thickening. Between 6 and 8 weeks, premyloblasts and myeloblasts which appear as sheet-like collection begin to extend to form five different laminae. [8] The orbicularis oris muscle develops from two embryonic laminae, the mandibular lamina (which differentiates partially into the lower fibers of the muscle) and the infraorbital lamina (which develops into the upper fibers of the muscle). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Optic Tract -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The optic tracts consist of the axons originating from the retinal ganglion cells (RGCs) in the retina. Axons of RGCs from throughout the retina join at the optic disc, an area of the retina devoid of photoreceptors colloquially referred to as the “blind spot.” [4] These unmyelinated fibers are referred to as the intraocular segment of the optic nerve. These fibers traverse the lamina cribrosa, an area of fenestrations in the sclera, and continue as the myelinated intraorbital segment of the optic nerve, CN II. [1] The intraorbital segment of CN II communicates directly with the dural lining and the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of the subarachnoid space, allowing for changes in intracranial pressure to manifest as optic nerve abnormalities. The intracanalicular segment of CN II exits the tendinous ring or annulus of Zinn, the common origin of the four extraocular rectus muscles, and the optic canal along with the ophthalmic artery. The optic canal is an obliquely oriented channel through the lesser wing of the sphenoid bone, opening into the bony skull base as the optic foramen. The intracranial optic nerve segment enters the middle cranial fossa and joins the contralateral optic nerve segment, forming the optic chiasm in the suprasellar cistern. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Scalp -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: As previously mentioned, the galea aponeurotica is a continuation of the occipitofrontal muscle. The occipitofrontalis muscle is split into the occipital and frontal bellies. The frontalis belly originates from the galea aponeurotica anteriorly and inserts into the superior orbicularis oculi at the level of the eyebrow. The occipitalis muscle origin is a posterior attachment at the mastoid and superior nuchal lines and inserts on the galea aponeurotica. These muscles are innervated by the superior zygomatic and posterior auricular nerve, respectively. They work in conjunction to pull the scalp back and elevate the eyebrows. The contraction of the frontalis muscle specifically creates wrinkles on the forehead. Of note, the loose connective tissue layer extends deep to the muscles. Bleeding into this space can track down to the orbicularis oculi causing a periorbital hematoma, more commonly known as a “black eye.” [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Inferior Oblique Muscle -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Around 7 to 8 weeks of gestation, the inferior oblique begins to form from a dense mesenchymal condensation near the infraorbital nerve. Unlike the extraocular rectus muscles, which have a common origin at the annulus of Zinn, the inferior oblique originates from a different region and lacks cartilaginous tissue at its origin. By 12 to 15 weeks of gestation, the inferior oblique's muscular origin appears, attaching to dense fibrous tissue and eventually merging with the posterior part of the sclera. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Eyelid -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The eyelid is derived from secondary mesenchyme and surface ectoderm. Secondary mesenchyme is produced when the cranial neural crest cells invade the mesoderm. The surface ectoderm gives rise to the eyelid skin epithelium, conjunctival, hair follicles and accessory glands. The mesenchyme gives rise to the tarsal plate, the muscles in the eyelid and the orbital septum. The eyelid begins to form during week 6 of gestation and continues to develop until nearly complete at 36 weeks. The stages of development are formation, fusion, development, separation, and maturation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck; Frontalis Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The frontalis muscle is made up of vertical striations in a fan-like distribution. Clinically, the muscle is sometimes divided into medial, intermediate and lateral fibers, although no anatomical or histological distinctions exist. It originates posteriorly from the galea aponeurotica, which corresponds with the hairline on the surface. Inferomedially, the muscle interdigitates with fibers of procerus muscle, while more inferolaterally, it has attachments to the orbicularis oculi and corrugator muscles. Generally, the frontalis inserts at the eyebrow dermis and terminates laterally at the temporal ridge, but there is some variance and occasionally may terminate more medially as well. [10] [11] While overall, it is a thin muscle with high vascularity, the bulk of it is located right above the brow. It is thinnest laterally, which represents an area of weakness and the first area to sag as we age.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Optic Cup -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Neuroectoderm cells comprise the optic cup. [9] While structures that are direct derivatives of the optic cup are in the above section, it is important to note the embryologic origin of other ocular structures. Surface ectoderm is responsible for the development of the lens, conjunctiva, lids, and corneal epithelium. The optic cup lip becomes the adult iris edge. Neural crest cells give rise to the corneal endothelium, trabecular meshwork (TM), scleral fibroblasts, connective fascia of extraocular muscles, and optic nerve meninges.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Skin, Superficial Musculoaponeurotic System (SMAS) Fascia -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The superficial facial musculature originates from the 2nd arch mesenchyme, which migrates during development and forms a premuscular lamina. This premuscular lamina ultimately gives rise to the mandibular, temporal, infraorbital, and cervical laminae during the 8th week of embryogenesis. The platysma originates from a part of the cervical lamina, which encloses the inferior aspects of the parotid gland and cheek. The SMAS derives from the area superior to this lamina. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Orbicularis Oculi Muscle -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The orbicularis oculi originates from the 2nd pharyngeal arch's local mesenchymal tissue and develops similarly to the extraocular muscles, evolving from mesenchymal cells to early myoblasts, myoblasts, myoblast fusion, myotubes, and ultimately, mature muscle. The orbicularis oculi initially forms as a sheet covering the lid's anterior surface. Afterward, the muscle differentiates into pretarsal, preseptal, and orbital parts by the 250-mm stage, dividing further as the upper and lower lids separate. The orbicularis oculi extends medially to attach to the medial and lateral palpebral ligaments. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Mid Forehead Brow Lift -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Many surgical procedures, such as cranial trephination, nasal reconstruction, and skin grafting, have been performed for hundreds of years, and some, like cataract surgery, thousands of years. Surprisingly, brow lift surgery was only reported in the 20th century when Lexer first discussed and presented the forehead lift in 1910. Subsequently, an early coronal brow lift was described by Hunt, who did not undermine any of the tissues, thus limiting results. Joseph, in 1931, presented a detailed description of the pretrichial brow lift as well as incisions made lower on the forehead to augment the brow elevation. Many surgeons continued the practice of simple tissue resection until Passot reported selective neurotomy of the frontal branch of the facial nerve in 1933. This method diminished forehead wrinkles; however, the resting tone of the frontalis muscle was lost, and this was clearly counterproductive for brow ptosis. For reasons not entirely clear, surgeons continued to explore the idea of forehead motor denervation. Edwards reported isolated temporal neurectomy as recently as 1957. A more anatomical approach was advanced by Bames that same year when he described a direct eyebrow lift. Through this approach, he weakened the corrugator muscles and undermined the forehead up to the hairline while crosshatching the frontalis muscle. Modern hairline and coronal approaches to the forehead lift and brow lift were ushered in by Pangman and Wallace in 1961. Further refinement occurred in 1962 when Gonzalez-Ulloa incorporated the forehead lift into his facelift procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Transconjunctival Blepharoplasty -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Applied Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The lower eyelid is supported medially by the medial canthal tendon and laterally by the lateral canthal tendon. At the tarsal plate level, the eyelid has a bi-lamellar structure with an anterior lamella of skin and orbicularis muscle with a suborbicularis fascial plane with minimal fat. The posterior lamella comprises the tarsus (which measures 1 mm in thickness and 5 to 6 mm in height) and conjunctiva. Below the level of the tarsal plate, the eyelid may be considered as having a tri-lamella structure with anterior lamella consisting of skin and orbicularis oculi muscle, the middle lamella comprising of the orbital septum, and to a degree, the lower eyelid retractors (called the capsulopalpebral fascia) with fat interposed between the septum and the retractors. At the orbital rim, the orbital septum fuses with the periosteum creating what is called the arcus marginalis.  The posterior lamella may be considered as consisting of, to a degree, the lower eyelid retractors and the conjunctiva. The lower eyelid retractors are analogous to the upper eyelid retractors (levator palpebrae superioris and its aponeurosis): the lower lid retractors arise from the inferior rectus muscle, envelope the inferior oblique muscle, and proceed to insert into the inferior border of the tarsal plate and orbital septum.  Like the levator palpebrae, the lower lid retractors are supplied by the oculomotor nerve. The equivalent of Muller's muscle (innervated by the sympathetic fibers originating from the carotid plexus) is a less defined muscle present just behind the capsulopalpebral fascia, often called the inferior tarsal smooth muscle. Similar to the Whitnall's ligament in the upper eyelid, Lockwood's ligament (the suspensory ligamentous sling supporting the globe) is attached laterally to the Whitnall's tubercle and inferolateral orbital rim and medially to the inferomedial orbital rim. Analogous to the upper eyelid post-levator aponeurosis fat pad, the lower eyelid has two additional thin layers of fat, the inter-lower eyelid retractor fat pad lying between the two layers of the lower eyelid retractors and the post-lower eyelid retractor fat pad lying between this and the conjunctiva. Whitnall’s tubercle is 3 to 4 mm inside the orbital rim and just above the horizontal axis. Into this tubercle, insert several structures: lateral canthal tendon, orbital septum, Lockwood’s ligament, Whitnall’s ligament, the deep head of orbicularis muscle, and the check ligaments of the lateral rectus muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Direct Brow Lift -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pertinent Anatomic Structures
retrieved_docs.content: Supraorbital nerve This nerve exits a notch or foramen roughly 2.7 cm lateral to the midline forehead between the midpupillary line and the medial limbus. The supraorbital nerve further divides into deep and superficial branches, providing sensory innervation to the frontoparietal and upper eyelid skin, respectively. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The upper lip is characterized by a symmetrical pair of paramedian vertical philtral ridges bordering the central depression known as the philtrum, directly below the nasal septum. The philtral ridges and the philtrum are formed by a unique collection of dermal collagen and dense elastic tissue. As elasticity is diminished with age, the philtrum takes on a less prominent appearance. The philtrum is believed to serve as a supply of additional skin to be recruited for oral movements requiring stretching of the upper lip. The inferior margin of the philtrum forms the downward arch of the cupid’s bow, while the underlying fleshy fullness is known as the tubercle or procheilon. Outlining the vermilion borders of the upper and lower lips is a 2 to 3 mm pale convexity known as the white roll, formed by the bulging of the orbicularis oris muscle lying beneath. The upper and lower lips connect to the gums by the frenulum labii superioris and frenulum labii inferioris, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Orbit Bones -- Introduction -- Orbital Foramina, Fissures, and Ducts
retrieved_docs.content: The optic nerve, the ophthalmic artery, and choroid plexus sympathetic fibers come from the middle cranial fossa and travel into the orbit via the optic foramen. The optic foramen passes through the lesser wing of the sphenoid bone and travels inferiorly, anteriorly, and laterally as it enters the orbit. The supraorbital foramen is on the superior margin of the orbit and contains blood vessels and the supraorbital nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 49
retrieved_docs.content: for oxygen support in these conditions. Ocular diagnostic procedures are organized under uncategorized histopathology procedures, a parent category that also includes anatomy, indicating that eye histopathology forms part of a broader anatomical and pathological assessment framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082
retrieved_docs.content: Moreover, diagnostic observations and pathological findings related to the choroidea are specifically localized to the site termed “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating a genetically related placental structure. Precise identification of choroidal involvement at this site is essential for accurate diagnosis, management, and prognostication of the diverse ocular diseases that arise within the choroidea.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: complications involve serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), general choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), and choroidal tear. Language‑specific eponymous entities also include familial choroidal membrane disorder (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية) and choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي). Collectively, these interrelated entities provide a comprehensive framework for diagnosing, classifying, and managing ocular, vascular, and systemic diseases that affect the choroid, retina, and related structures, emphasizing the choroidea as the common locus for inflammatory, degenerative, hemorrhagic, depositional, traumatic, postoperative, metastatic, and scar‑forming processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2128
retrieved_docs.content: taxonomy aids clinicians in recognizing the diverse presentations of choroidal and retinal pathology, facilitating accurate diagnosis, targeted management, and coordinated patient care across a range of ocular disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is identified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), representing bleeding that originates from the front portion of the nasal cavity and is commonly associated with structural or mucosal abnormalities of the septum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2055
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the eye are classified as a subcategory of histopathology procedures that are not placed in any other specific category, indicating that eye‑related diagnostic methods fall under the broader, uncategorized group of histopathological examinations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 266

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 238
retrieved_docs.content: location for open wounds of the head, neck, and trunk and is the focus of “other therapeutic procedures on eyelids; conjunctiva; cornea.” Therapeutic ear and skin procedures such as “Opening of skin” and “Unspecified elective surgery for purposes other than remedying health states” are grouped under “other therapeutic ear procedures,” illustrating the overlap between minor surgical interventions across these regions. Overall, the integrated classification demonstrates how specific interventions, diagnostic findings, personal‑history elements, and coding schemas converge on the ear, skin, subcutaneous tissue, and eyelid anatomy while also encompassing sexual health and residual medical coding domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 610
retrieved_docs.content: (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), is recognized as a distinct manifestation within this classification. Together, these entities illustrate diverse etiologies—vascular, inflammatory, traumatic, postoperative, degenerative, and genetic—that affect the choroid and retina, underscoring the importance of precise classification for diagnosis, management, and prognostication in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2690
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of tarsal gland is a specialized form of incision of skin, representing a more specific surgical action that targets the tarsal gland within the eyelid. Because it involves the eyelid structures, this procedure is also classified as one of the other therapeutic procedures on eyelids, conjunctiva, and cornea. Consequently, incision of tarsal gland bridges the general category of skin incisions with the broader group of ocular therapeutic interventions, highlighting its role in both dermatologic and ophthalmologic surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: The category “إجراءات هيستوباثولوجيا، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (histopathology procedures not classified elsewhere) functions as a broad parent classification for specialized medical procedures. Within this hierarchy, “diagnostic procedures on eye” are identified as a child of this parent, indicating that eye‑focused diagnostic methods are grouped under the umbrella of unclassified histopathology procedures. Likewise, “التشريح” (anatomical or dissection procedures) is also listed as a child of the same parent category, showing that both eye diagnostic procedures and anatomical/dissection procedures share the same higher‑level classification. This structured relationship clarifies how specific diagnostic and anatomical interventions are organized within broader histopathology categories, supporting accurate documentation, coding, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: حصوي (calcific degeneration of choroidal or retinal tissue), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal atrophy) and حثل المشيمية حول الحُليلة، جزئي (partial sclerosis of the choroid surrounding the macula). Together, these entities illustrate the extensive range of inflammatory, vascular, hemorrhagic, degenerative, and structural pathologies classified under Disorders of choroid and retina, underscoring the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.
